title: Natural dye journal and record keeping
subtitle: How to record dye experiments so that successful colours can be reproduced and failures understood
excerpt: Natural dyeing produces variable results: the same plant, the same mordant, and the same recipe can give noticeably different colours in different seasons, with different water, or on different fibre preparations. A consistent dye journal is the only way to understand why the colour changed and how to reproduce a result you liked. This reading explains what to record and how to organise the records.
sourceNotes: Natural dye record-keeping practice synthesised from general natural dyeing references. The specific data fields recommended reflect standard practice in dye documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The dry weight of the fibre dyed, used as the reference for all recipe quantities. Recording WOF with every entry allows recipes to be scaled accurately.

A mineral salt applied before dyeing to bond the dye to the fibre. Recording the mordant type and percentage is essential for reproducing a colour: the same plant with different mordants gives completely different colours.
A small addition of iron, copper, or acid to the dye bath or after-bath that shifts the final colour. Modifiers account for some of the largest colour changes between otherwise identical batches.

A measure of how well a dyed colour resists fading under light exposure. Recording lightfastness by leaving a small test strip in a sunny window for several months gives a practical assessment of long-term colour stability.

content.text: The difficulty with natural dyeing is not the chemistry but the variability. A batch of weld that gave a brilliant yellow in June gives a muted gold in September from the same plant. The same recipe produces different tones in hard water and soft water. A poor
mordant

step wastes both the plant material and the dyeing session. A dye journal captures the variables so that when something goes wrong you can identify which variable changed, and when something goes right you know how to repeat it.
What to record
The fibre
Record the fibre type (wool, silk, cotton), the breed or origin if known, and the dry
weight of fibre

(WOF). Note whether the fibre was pre-washed, pre-mordanted, or used fresh from the skein. Fibre preparation affects dye uptake: a skein that has been stored in lanolin-rich conditions takes colour differently from a clean, degreased one.
The mordant

Record the mordant type, the percentage of WOF used, and whether cream of tartar or another assistant was added. Note the mordant temperature and the duration of the mordant bath. A mordant step cut short by 15 minutes produces noticeably less uptake in the dye step.
The plant material

Record the plant species (Latin name where possible), the plant part used (flowers, leaves, bark, roots), the date and location of gathering, and whether the material was fresh or dried. Note the percentage of WOF: 100% fresh weight gives different results from 100% dry weight, and the two are not equivalent. Record whether the dye bath was prepared fresh or from a stored extract.
The dye process
Record the extraction temperature and time, the dyeing temperature and time, and the water source (tap, collected rain, filtered). Note any
modifier
used: iron, copper, or an acid rinse. Record the modifier percentage and at what stage it was applied (in the dye bath, in an after-bath, or in the final rinse).
The result

Attach a small wound swatch of the dyed yarn directly to the journal page with a needle or glue. Describe the colour precisely. A note like "warm amber-gold, similar to dried straw" is far more useful than a single word. Record the
lightfastness

by pinning a second small swatch in a south-facing window for 2-3 months. Photograph the original swatch next to the faded one. This practical lightfastness test is more useful than published ratings, which are measured under laboratory conditions that rarely match home dyeing.
Format and organisation

A simple notebook works well. Number each entry and use consistent headings for every field so records are comparable across sessions. Index by plant species on the inside back cover. If you prefer a digital record, a spreadsheet with one row per session allows sorting by plant, mordant, or colour result. In either format, the physical swatch attached to the record is irreplaceable: a written colour description is always less accurate than looking at the actual yarn.

items.problem: A recipe that worked well last season gives a very different colour this time
items.cause: The plant gathering season, the water source, or the mordant percentage changed between the two batches.
items.fix: Compare the two journal entries line by line. Check the plant gathering date, the mordant percentage, and the water source. In hard-water areas, even switching from tap water to bottled water can shift the colour.

items.problem: The journal entries are becoming too complex to be useful
items.cause: Too many variables were changed at once in a single session
items.fix: Change only one variable per session deliberately. Use the previous successful batch as the control and vary only the mordant, or only the plant quantity, or only the modifier. This makes the record interpretable.

items.problem: The attached swatches are fading or falling off the journal pages over time
items.cause: The swatches were attached with glue that deteriorated, or the journal is kept in a bright location
items.fix: Sew swatches to the page with a few stitches rather than gluing. Store the journal away from light. For long-term reference, scan or photograph the swatch on the day of dyeing as a backup record.
